entry.title: Elevate Your SAC Dashboards: Unleash the Power of Custom R Visualizations!
entry.content:
In the world of business intelligence and analytics, visual storytelling is a powerful tool for uncovering insights and driving informed decision-making. SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), with its robust dashboarding and analytics capabilities, stands as a leader in this domain. But what if you need to go beyond its native visualizations? Enter R, the widely used programming language for statistical computing and graphics.

By combining the flexibility of R's visualization libraries with SAC's interactive dashboards, you can create custom widgets that elevate your analytics game. From dynamic heatmaps to intricate network diagrams, R empowers you to craft visuals that cater to unique business requirements, enabling advanced storytelling and better decision support through widgets, such as Gauge Charts, in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC). We’ll guide you through the setup process, demonstrate a practical example using ggplot2, and share best practices for seamlessly integrating R-powered widgets into SAC dashboards. Whether you’re a data scientist, business analyst, or SAC user looking to push the boundaries of analytics, this guide will unlock new opportunities to enhance your SAC dashboards with custom, dynamic visualizations.

 Let’s dive in and transform your analytics experience!

Set Up R Integration in SAC

Before you can start creating R-powered charts, you need to ensure that SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) is properly connected to your R environment. This integration is essential for running R scripts and embedding custom visualizations like Gauge Charts into your SAC dashboards.

  1. Access SAC:
  • Log in to your SAP Analytics Cloud account: Ensure you have the necessary access rights to create stories, dashboards, and use custom widgets in SAC.
  1. Activate R Script Integration:
  • Go to the SAC Administration Page:
    • Navigate to System > Administration > Connectivity.

 

 

    • Here, check and ensure R integration is available and enabled.
  • Ensure the R Environment is Ready:
    • SAC requires an external R server or R environment to execute the scripts.
    • Make sure that R packages like ggplot2, plotly, or any other visualization libraries are installed on your R environment, as these will be used to create custom charts.
  1. Verify Configuration:
  • Check Connection Details: Ensure that SAC is correctly configured to connect with the R server.
  • If possible, run a test script from SAC to confirm that the R integration is working properly.

Here’s a step-by-step guide to creating and using a custom R-Widget in your SAC Story.

Step 1: Add a Custom Widget:

  • Click on + Add or Insert in the Story workspace.
  • Select Others => R Visualization.

 

 

Step 2: On the Builder pane,

  • Choose + Add Input Data, where you can select the model from where the data needs to be used for the widget
  • You have the flexibility to choose Input from more than one model

 

 

  • Double click on the model name and Select the Measures and Dimensions to be used in the widget from the Builder.
  • Using the preview option(Expand the window by clicking the below icon on the top right)

 

 

Step 3: On the Builder pane,

  • Choose + Edit Script, where we will write the R script for Gauge meter/Taco meter
  • The Script Tab for an R Widget is divided into four sections, each serving a specific purpose to configure and execute the widget. Here's an overview of these sections
  1. Editor is where you write and manage your R script.
  2. The Environment section displays the objects (e.g., dataframes, variables) created during the script execution.
  3. Console shows real-time outputs, logs, and error messages generated by your R script.
  4. The Preview section renders the output of your R script, such as charts or visualizations.

 

  • On the Editor space use the below R-Script for Gauge meter,
  •  

library(plotly)

library(ggplot2)

# Create the Gauge Chart using Plotly

fig <- plot_ly(

  domain = list(x = c(0, 10000), y = c(0, 10000)),  # Define the size of the plot

  value = Model1$Count,  # Current value from the data model (replace Model1$Count with actual value)

  title = list(text = "Sales Performance", font = list(size = 10)),  # Title of the gauge

  type = "indicator",  # Define the chart type as "indicator" for the gauge

  mode = "gauge+number",  # Display both the gauge and the number

  number = list(font = list(color = "black", size = 20)),  # Number formatting in the center of the gauge

  gauge = list(

    # Set the gauge axis with range limits and tick values

    axis = list(

      range = c(0, MD_XXXX$Sales_Target_Count_Total),  # Define the range for the gauge axis

      tickwidth = 1,  # Set the width of ticks

      tickcolor = "darkblue",  # Set the tick color

      tickmode = "array",  # Use array for ticks

      tickvals = c(0, MD_XXXXX$`Sales Target Ct-YTD`, MD_XXXXX$Sales_Target_Count_Total)  # Define the tick values

    ),

    bar = list(color = "black"),  # Set the color of the gauge bar

    steps = list(

      # Define color-coded steps for different performance ranges

      list(range = c(0, (MD_XXXXX$Sales_Target_Count_Total * 30 / 100)), color = "Red"),  # Red step (below 30% target)

      list(range = c((MD_XXXXX$Sales_Target_Count_Total * 30 / 100), (MD_XXXXX$Sales_Target_Count_Total * 65 / 100)), color = "Yellow"),  # Yellow step (30-65% target)

      list(range = c((MD_XXXXXX$Sales_Target_Count_Total * 65 / 100), MD_XXXXX$Sales_Target_Count_Total), color = "Green")  # Green step (above 65% target)

    ),

    threshold = list(

      line = list(color = "Limegreen", width = 4),  # Define the threshold line with color and width

      thickness = 0.75,  # Set the thickness of the threshold line

      value = MD_XXXXX$`Sales Target Ct-YTD`  # The threshold value (YTD sales target)

    )

  )

)

 

# Adjust layout (margins, etc.)

fig <- fig %>%

  layout(

    margin = list(l = 20, r = 50, t = 10, b = 10) 

  )

# Display the gauge chart

Fig

 

 

Step 3: After adding the script, click Run or Execute to run the R script.

  • The Gauge Meter should now be displayed with the values and colors dynamically set based on the data in the input model.

Value: The gauge will display the current value (e.g., Model1$Count) in the center of the chart.

Thresholds and Colors: Based on the Sales Target, the gauge will show color-coded sections:

  • Red for values below 30% of the target.
  • Yellow for values between 30% and 65% of the target.
  • Green for values above 65% of the target.

 Dynamic Updates: The gauge will dynamically update based on the values from the data model, ensuring that your visualizations stay accurate and up-to-date.

 

 

  •  If needed, adjust the layout, margins, and colors to match your desired visual theme.

One of the powerful features of SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) is its ability to dynamically link custom R widgets like your Gauge Meter to page or story filters, as well as perform linked analysis from other tables or charts. This makes the widget even more interactive and responsive to user input.

By the end of this blog, you will have a clear understanding of how to integrate R-powered visualizations like Gauge meter into your SAC dashboards, and how to leverage the interactive features like filters and linked analysis. This will enable you to create more dynamic, user-driven dashboards that provide deeper insights and a more engaging analytics experience.

entry.title: Calling SOAP Asynchronous Api In RAP ( Public Cloud )
entry.content:
Greetings to everyone,

I recently took part in my first public cloud project. I did a lot of research and development in this process. I would like to share with you a case that happened to me and the steps I applied in this case.

Used Api: Journal Entry - Clearing (Asynchronous) 

( https://api.sap.com/api/JOURNALENTRYBULKCLEARINGREQUES/overview )

Tutorial on the use of SOAP Services:

https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/abap-environment-soap-web-services..html

Case: Journal Entry - Clearing (Asynchronous) api is called with a button behind a screen made using RAP. In this case, when we start with the approach in the tutorial link above, dump is taken on the screen caused by the modify statement in the automatically generated class.

(  https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-rap/rap-transactional-model-and-sap-luw )

Approach: In this case, we can run the xml of the soap service by embedding it into the body as if we call ODATA service. However, ws-a addressing and generate message id ticks must be checked in order to run Asynchronous Soap services. The aim is to make these ticks behave as if they are checked in postman and to provide the same situation in the code.

In order to run the Asynchronous SOAP service via SOAPUI, the following two ticks must be checked ( https://www.soapui.org/docs/soap-and-wsdl/supported-standards/using-ws-addressing/ )

In order for Postman to correspond to this, we need to put the ws-a addressing link that we view in the wsdl file into the header and then fill in the action and message id parts.

Action: We can get the action link on SoapUI.

WS-A: We can get the link on WSDL.

MessageID: urn:uuid:{{{$randomUUUID}}} should be used to structure that generate random uuid with every time we do a post operation.

We can embed this structure into our transform object when calling the api from our Behavior class. We can use cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_c36_static( ) method to provide the same format as urn:uuid:{{$randomUUUID}}.

The code of the Behavior Class Method where the Asynchronous Soap API is called:

 

TRY.
DATA(lo_http_destination) = cl_http_destination_provider=>create_by_url( 'https://host-api.s4hana.cloud.sap/sap/bc/srt/scs_ext/sap/journalentrybulkclearingreques' ).
  CATCH cx_http_dest_provider_error INTO DATA(lo_http_dest_provider_error).
ENDTRY.

  TRY.
        DATA(lo_web_http_client) = cl_web_http_client_manager=>create_by_http_destination( lo_http_destination ) .
      CATCH  cx_web_http_client_error INTO DATA(lo_client_error).
        DATA(lv_client) = lo_client_error->get_text( ).
    ENDTRY.

    DATA(lo_web_http_request) = lo_web_http_client->get_http_request( ).
    lo_web_http_request->set_authorization_basic(  EXPORTING  i_username = Communication User
                                                              i_password = Communication User Password ).


    lo_web_http_request->set_header_fields( VALUE #( (  name = 'Content-Type' value = 'text/xml; charset=UTF-8' )
                                                     (  name = 'SOAPAction'    value = 'http://sap.com/xi/SAPSCORE/SFIN/JournalEntryBulkClearingRequest_In/JournalEntryBulkClearingRequest_InRequest' ) )   ).
TRY.
        DATA(lv_uuid) = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_c36_static( ).
      CATCH cx_uuid_error INTO DATA(lx_uuid).
    ENDTRY.
    lv_uuid_s = lv_uuid.


*--->fill the body


  DATA(lv_body) = xco_cp_json=>data->from_abap( ls_body )->apply( VALUE #(  ( xco_cp_json=>transformation->underscore_to_pascal_case ) ) )->to_string( ).

        TRY.
            CALL TRANSFORMATION zfi_ac_api_to
           SOURCE data = ls_body
            RESULT XML lv_body.
          CATCH cx_root INTO DATA(lo_root).
            DATA(lv_mes_tr) = lo_root->get_text( ).
        ENDTRY.


        REPLACE ALL OCCURRENCES OF 'uuid:{UUID}' IN lv_body WITH lv_uuid_s.
        REPLACE ALL OCCURRENCES OF '<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-16"?>' IN lv_body WITH ''.
        REPLACE ALL OCCURRENCES OF '<' IN lv_body WITH '<'.
        REPLACE ALL OCCURRENCES OF '>' IN lv_body WITH '>'.

        lo_web_http_request->set_text( lv_body ).
        TRY.
            DATA(lo_web_http_response) = lo_web_http_client->execute( if_web_http_client=>post ).
          CATCH cx_web_http_client_error INTO lo_client_error.
            lv_client = lo_client_error->get_text( ).
        ENDTRY.
        DATA(lv_response)      = lo_web_http_response->get_text( ).
        DATA(ls_response_stat) = lo_web_http_response->get_status( ).

 

The code of the Transformation Object:

 

<xsl:transform xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform" xmlns:sap="http://www.sap.com/sapxsl" version="1.0">
  <xsl:strip-space elements="*"/>

  <xsl:output encoding="UTF-8" indent="yes" method="xml"/>

  <xsl:template match="DATA">
    <soapenv:Envelope xmlns:soapenv="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/envelope/" xmlns:sfin="http://sap.com/xi/SAPSCORE/SFIN">
      <soapenv:Header xmlns:wsa="http://www.w3.org/2005/08/addressing">
        <wsa:Action soapenv:mustUnderstand="1">http://sap.com/xi/SAPSCORE/SFIN/JournalEntryBulkClearingRequest_In/JournalEntryBulkClearingRequest_InRequest</wsa:Action>
        <wsa:MessageID soapenv:mustUnderstand="1">uuid:{UUID}</wsa:MessageID>

      </soapenv:Header>
      <soapenv:Body>
        <sfin:JournalEntryBulkClearingRequest>
          <xsl:element name="MessageHeader">
            <xsl:element name="ID">
              <xsl:value-of select="JOURNAL_ENTRY_BULK_CLEARING_RE/MESSAGE_HEADER/I_D"/>
            </xsl:element>
            <xsl:element name="CreationDateTime">
              <xsl:value-of select="JOURNAL_ENTRY_BULK_CLEARING_RE/MESSAGE_HEADER/CREATION_DATE_TIME"/>
            </xsl:element>
            <TestDataIndicator>
              <xsl:value-of select="JOURNAL_ENTRY_BULK_CLEARING_RE/MESSAGE_HEADER/TEST_DATA_INDICATOR"/>
            </TestDataIndicator>
          </xsl:element>
          <JournalEntryClearingRequest>
            <xsl:element name="MessageHeader">
              <ID>
                <xsl:value-of select="JOURNAL_ENTRY_BULK_CLEARING_RE/JOURNAL_ENTRY_CLEARING_REQUEST/MESSAGE_HEADER/I_D"/>
              </ID>
              <xsl:element name="CreationDateTime">
                <xsl:value-of select="JOURNAL_ENTRY_BULK_CLEARING_RE/JOURNAL_ENTRY_CLEARING_REQUEST/MESSAGE_HEADER/CREATION_DATE_TIME"/>
              </xsl:element>
            </xsl:element>
            
            <JournalEntry>
              <CompanyCode>
                <xsl:value-of select="JOURNAL_ENTRY_BULK_CLEARING_RE/JOURNAL_ENTRY_CLEARING_REQUEST/JOURNAL_ENTRY/COMPANY_CODE"/>
              </CompanyCode>
              <AccountingDocumentType>
                <xsl:value-of select="JOURNAL_ENTRY_BULK_CLEARING_RE/JOURNAL_ENTRY_CLEARING_REQUEST/JOURNAL_ENTRY/ACCOUNTING_DOCUMENT_TYPE"/>
              </AccountingDocumentType>
              <DocumentDate>
                <xsl:value-of select="JOURNAL_ENTRY_BULK_CLEARING_RE/JOURNAL_ENTRY_CLEARING_REQUEST/JOURNAL_ENTRY/DOCUMENT_DATE"/>
              </DocumentDate>
              <PostingDate>
                <xsl:value-of select="JOURNAL_ENTRY_BULK_CLEARING_RE/JOURNAL_ENTRY_CLEARING_REQUEST/JOURNAL_ENTRY/POSTING_DATE"/>
              </PostingDate>
              <CurrencyCode>
                <xsl:value-of select="JOURNAL_ENTRY_BULK_CLEARING_RE/JOURNAL_ENTRY_CLEARING_REQUEST/JOURNAL_ENTRY/CURRENCY_CODE"/>
              </CurrencyCode>
              <DocumentHeaderText>
                <xsl:value-of select="JOURNAL_ENTRY_BULK_CLEARING_RE/JOURNAL_ENTRY_CLEARING_REQUEST/JOURNAL_ENTRY/DOCUMENT_HEADER_TEXT"/>
              </DocumentHeaderText>
              <CreatedByUser>
                <xsl:value-of select="JOURNAL_ENTRY_BULK_CLEARING_RE/JOURNAL_ENTRY_CLEARING_REQUEST/JOURNAL_ENTRY/CREATED_BY_USER"/>
              </CreatedByUser>
              
              <xsl:for-each select="JOURNAL_ENTRY_BULK_CLEARING_RE/JOURNAL_ENTRY_CLEARING_REQUEST/JOURNAL_ENTRY/APARITEMS/item">
                <APARItems>
                  <ReferenceDocumentItem>
                    <xsl:value-of select="REFERENCE_DOCUMENT_ITEM"/>
                  </ReferenceDocumentItem>
                  <CompanyCode>
                    <xsl:value-of select="COMPANY_CODE"/>
                  </CompanyCode>
                  <AccountType>
                    <xsl:value-of select="ACCOUNT_TYPE"/>
                  </AccountType>
                  <APARAccount>
                    <xsl:value-of select="APARACCOUNT"/>
                  </APARAccount>
                  <FiscalYear>
                    <xsl:value-of select="FISCAL_YEAR"/>
                  </FiscalYear>
                  <AccountingDocument>
                    <xsl:value-of select="ACCOUNTING_DOCUMENT"/>
                  </AccountingDocument>
                  <AccountingDocumentItem>
                    <xsl:value-of select="ACCOUNTING_DOCUMENT_ITEM"/>
                  </AccountingDocumentItem>
                  <xsl:value-of select="OTHER_DEDUCTION_AMOUNT_IN_DSP"/>
                </APARItems>

              </xsl:for-each>
            </JournalEntry>
          </JournalEntryClearingRequest>
        </sfin:JournalEntryBulkClearingRequest>
      </soapenv:Body>
    </soapenv:Envelope>
  </xsl:template>
</xsl:transform>

 

I hope this blog post has been helpful. 

Have a great day!

entry.title: AIF Automatic Reprocessing – Technical deep dive
entry.content:
Application interface framework (AIF) AIF Documentation  is an framework which is widely used in S/4 HANA, Central finance for rationalization of different kind of interfaces with universal monitoring and error handling capabilities, underlying interface technology e.g. IDOC, XML etc remains abstract to the end user (Technical / Business).

With growing use-cases of AIF in large enterprise setting, there comes different challenges and one of them is understanding of automatic reprocessing capabilities.

In this blog, we would deep dive into details of technical know-how of automatic reprocessing.

Use case – We have set up XML/Structured persistence interface in AIF and based on a specific message number, AIF should do the automatic processing in certain interval for certain times.

First Tcode which is used in this process is /n/AIF/REP_AC_DEF where reprocessing action is defined. SAP has delivered standard function module ‘/AIF/RESTART_MSG’ for this purpose which is sufficient in almost all the cases however if you have a custom requirement then custom FM based on template FM ‘/AIF/TEMPL_RESTART_AUTO_REPR’ can be created and assigned in this Tcode.

Assigned FM would be used during reprocessing job, it will pick the properties/action/logic from original interface configuration for which GUID is being reprocessed.

Assigned runtime config Id(Tcode - /n/AIF/PERS_CGR) determines how the reprocessing should occur, e.g. packet size, server group etc.

The automatic reprocessing is triggered by specific error messages for a registered AIF interface.

So the user has to register the AIF Interface and the corresponding error messages by message class and number for automatic reprocessing in the transaction /n/AIF/REP_AC_ASGN.

 

  1. Assign the reprocessing action which was defined in previous transaction.
  2. Max repr. Counter - Define a maximum number of reprocessing attempts that can be executed for a failed data message.
  3. Minimum wait time (Seconds / Minutes /  Hours) - Define a minimum waiting time that must pass before the application can trigger a reprocessing attempt. 
    e.g. If a vendor is blocked and it takes around 6 hours for master data team to unblock the vendor then Minimum waiting time can be defined as 6 hours so that reprocessing can be attempted after vendor is unblocked.
  4.  Maximum wait time (Seconds / Minutes /  Hours) - Define a maximum waiting time before which             application must trigger a reprocessing attempt.
    e.g. if you are supposed to provide a acknowledgment of original AIF message with in 10 hours then maximum waiting time could be 10 hours so that all reprocessing attempts can be made to get the latest status based on the error.
  5. Intermediate status (In Process or Error) the failed data message displays while it's waiting to be reprocessed.

Conclusion –

  1. First Reprocessing iteration should start only after “Min time” + original AIF message creation time has elapsed but No later than “Max time” + original AIF message creation time
  2. Next retry attempt of reprocessing job would occur after previous iteration completion time + “Max time” , if same errors still exists

Below FM / class method are called during original AIF message processing if the AIF error message meets the reprocessing criteria and it schedules the job based on defined reprocessing config.

 

 

Once above is executed, a job is created in ‘Scheduled’ status and can be checked in SM37. Planned start date for these jobs are defined based on Min and Max time defined in AIF reprocessing config.

Job name - /AIF/Namespace_RuntimeConfigID*

Reprocessing data is captured in table /AIF/REP_MSG_CNT which provides valuable information like how many retry attempts are made , timestamp etc.

Working example – Lets check how this config works with a working example-

We have set the config as below where maximum retries are 2, Minimum waiting time is 1 Minute and Maximum waiting time is 10 Mis.

Chain of events-

  1. Original AIF message was created on 15:38 which would schedule reprocessing job.
  2. First reprocessing job was released with a scheduled start time of 15:48 , the original message was created on 15:38 however ‘Max time’ was defined as 10 mins so reprocessing jobs has been scheduled for original + 10 mins.
  3. Second reprocessing was released with a scheduled start time of 15:58 since first iteration was completed on 15:48 so first iteration + 10 Mins.

AIF Message job log with timings.

While AIF message is waiting to be reprocessed, the status becomes ‘In Progress’ and sub status would be ‘Automatic reprocessing’ which can be checked in corresponding index table.

 

Tips –

  1. Reprocessing Configuration performed for defining actions and assigning message is not transportable and should be done in each client separately.
  2. If there is an existing job scheduled between the minimum waiting time and the maximum waiting time, the reprocessing message is assigned to this job insteda of creating a new job.
  3. Runtime Configuration Group should be active and use Run Scheduled.

entry.title: Now Available: SAP Central Business Configuration Learning Journey with System Access
entry.content:
We’re thrilled to announce the launch of a brand-new learning journey! This blog post will provide you with all the important details about it.

The new learning journey enables you to configure business processes in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition with SAP Central Business Configuration and includes access to a practice system.

Check it out below 👇:

Configuring Business Processes in SAP S/4HANA Cloud with SAP Central Business Configuration

Landing page of the new learning journey

This learning journey is now available for SAP partners and customers, offering:

  • Comprehensive self-paced learning content covering key CBC topics
  • Hands-on system access for practical experience
  • Essential skills to configure business processes in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition using SAP Central Business Configuration

Hands-on Practice section as part of the new learning journey

Key Learning Objectives:

  • Setting up the implementation workspaces for the development system
  • Defining the scope and country/region localizations
  • Selecting the primary finance settings and building the organizational structure
  • Defining and transporting product-specific configuration values
  • Creating users and granting access to the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition development system
  • Creating users and granting access to the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition test and production systems

Don’t miss this opportunity to enhance your expertise and feel free to share the details within your network. Explore now and start your journey today!

Make sure to follow the SAP Central Business Configuration tag or follow this dedicated blog collection to stay up to date with the latest blog posts.

entry.title: Re: Use of S-Gen after Upgrade in SAP
entry.content:
Please use the official name everywhere. Nobody knows "S-Gen", nor "SGen", it's SGEN, the transaction code.

It's not "used in SAP to regenerate or upgrade the SAP Kernel and Data Dictionary objects":

  1. it's not used to upgrade, it's (optionally) used after an upgrade.
  2. it doesn't regenerate the "SAP Kernel and Data Dictionary objects", it regenerates the ABAP Load (of programs).

Quick search of "SGEN" "blog" site:sap.com - Google Search also gives this third blog post: SGEN - An Overview - SAP Community.

Also found this one: SGEN - Search Results | SAP Help Portal.

Any reason to create this post which seems to be repeating many parts of the two other blog posts? Why not just adding NEW information?

entry.title: Re: Slow message processing sometimes - NAST
entry.content:
Please correct me if I didnt get it... As I can see trigerring message processing by application (completion) is ERUHR ("Date on which status record was created"). Time when messages processing has ended and selected media will be used for output = UHRVR ("Processing time").

Example:

At 14:47:04 the MAIL was created and sent (SOST), but it took 3 minutes...

entry.title: SAPLogon.exe is missing in C:\programme(x86)\SAP\FrontEnd\SAPgui
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

I have encountered a problem. When I install SAP 8.00 32bit on Windows 10, the installation completes successfully and everything works fine. The log files do not show any error codes.

However, the issue is that I cannot find SAP Logon when searching in the Start menu of Windows.

Instead, I have to navigate to C:\Program Files (x86)\SAP\FrontEnd\SAPgui and manually start saplgpad.exe to work.

After some research, I found that SAPLogon.exe and saplgpad.exe essentially serve the same purpose to launch the logon, but saplgpad.exe does not offer the option for variable logins.

For the rollout of the new SAP GUI 8 in our company, we need to have the SAP Logon both as a desktop shortcut and available in the Windows search bar.

I have already created a shortcut to saplgpad.exe for myself, but this is not a viable solution for the rollout, as every user would need to do the same.

We are talking about more than 400 users.

I appreciate any support or suggestions for a solution.

Best regards,

entry.title: Community edition Web build error.
entry.content:
Hello SAP Build Apps Community,

I hope you're all doing well! We’ve been using the SAP Build Apps Community Edition to create an MVP for our project management platform. Overall, our experience has been quite positive, but we’ve encountered a few bumps along the way. Recently, we've been running into an issue with the web version, and I wanted to reach out for some help.

Here’s the error we’re facing:

```
AB version: 3.8.0 COMMAND: USER_METERING_ENABLED=true SHOW_SUBSCRIPTION_VERIFICATION_NOTIFICATION=false SUBSCRIPTION_VERIFICATION_NOTIFICATION_DISMISSIBLE=true NEXT_SUPPORT_RELATIVE_PATHS=true NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=12240 npxrnv export -p web -s standalone -c standalone --ci --yes --packageManager yarn FAILED with ERROR: Command failed with exit code 1: npx rnv export -p web -s standalone -c standalone --ci --yes --packageManager yarn

```

Interestingly, this error doesn’t appear in a lightweight test project with fewer components, so it seems to be isolated to more complex builds.

I remember encountering a similar error about a year and a half ago, and at the time, it was mentioned that the build service had "run out of allocated memory." Given this, I’m wondering if the issue might be the same now or if something else is causing it. Any thoughts or advice would be greatly appreciated!

To give you more context, our app project (after export) is around 3.9 MB, and the last successful build was almost 14 MB. We’re working with several custom JavaScript files, a lot of logic nodes, and many expressions.

Has anyone else run into this problem? If so, how did you resolve it? And if this is a known issue, could you share an estimated timeline for when it might be fixed?

Thanks so much in advance for any feedback!

Best regards,
Mihály

entry.content:
Sometimes the ABAP Eclipse ADT code completion (Ctrl + Space) becomes lost.

With the below code, I obtain this crazy code completion proposal after the text "cl_abap_format=>" of the last line:

In the first occurrence, it works fine:

Code to reproduce:

 

REPORT z_reproduce_bug.
" code completion after cl_abap_format=> is wrong in the last line
DATA(v1) = escape( val    = sy-abcde
                   format = cl_abap_format=>e_string_tpl ).
DATA(v2) = |\n|.
DATA(v3) = escape( val    = sy-abcde
                   format = cl_abap_format=>e_string_tpl ).

 

No fix found in SAP notes.

System to reproduce the bug:

  • ADT 3.46
  • ABAP backend 7.40 SP8

It works fine with ADT 3.46 and backend 7.52 SP04.

entry.title: Step-by-Step Migration: From SAP Web IDE (Neo) to BAS (CF)
entry.content:
With the end of SAP Web IDE (Neo) coming soon, it's crucial to move your development projects to SAP Business Application Studio (CF). This guide will help you migrate your Fiori project quickly and easily, ensuring you can continue developing without any hiccups.

Prerequisites:

Ensure that the required destination is created in Cloud Foundry within your BAS subaccount.

Step 1: Exporting and Extracting the Project

  • Export your project from SAP Web IDE.
  • Extract the downloaded project file.

 

 Step 2:Setting Up the Development Space

  • In SAP BAS, create a new development space or use an existing one.

 

Step 3:Importing the Project

  • Drag and drop the extracted project file into the project pane in SAP BAS.
  • A popup will automatically appear to open the migration tool.

Manually Opening the Migration Tool

  • If needed, press Ctrl + Shift + P and search for "Fiori migration tool" to open it manually.

 

Step 4:Starting the Migration

  • Select the appropriate system, destination, and SAPUI5 version.
  • Click on "Start Migration".

 

Step 5:Installing Dependencies

  • Ensure that all dependencies listed in the package.json file are installed.
  • If there are any errors in the package.json, install the missing dependencies to resolve them.

 

Step 6:Previewing & Deploying the Application

  • Run ‘npm run start’ in the terminal to preview the application.
  • To deploy the application, you must create a deployment configuration from the application information section.

Common Issues:

 Resolving Theme/CSS Issues

  • If there are theme/CSS issues, search for the theme (e.g., sap_fiori_3) in the migrated project and replace it with a compatible theme for your SAP S/4HANA system.
  • Refresh the preview page to see the changes.

Data Flow Issues:

  • If the metadata isn't being called correctly, Follow the below steps
  1. Open Application Info.
  2. Navigate to Manage Service Models.
  3. Select the appropriate destination.
  4. Click Refresh & Save.
  5. You must stop and restart the preview to see the service changes.

Note: Ensure all dependencies are installed as per the package.json file. If there are any errors, install the missing dependencies to fix them.

entry.title: Field Service Technician Persona in SAP Maintenance Assistant
entry.content:
We’re thrilled to announce the latest innovations in our journey towards a comprehensive mobile solution for maintenance and field service execution. With the introduction of the Field Service Technician persona in the SAP Maintenance Assistant mobile app, we take a significant step forward in delivering a truly persona-centric platform.

Built on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) Mobile Services and integrated with SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, the SAP Maintenance Assistant mobile app now caters to multiple user roles—starting with technicians in the field. By embracing a standardized, unified technology stack, we ensure consistency, reliability, and scalability across different personas and business scenarios.

Through this new release, we’re continue to refine our mobile offerings, empower field service operations, and shape the future of Asset and Service Management on the SAP platform.

Multi-Persona Support and Licensing

Each persona receives tailored features and workflows that directly address their specific responsibilities. For field service technicians, this means streamlined work order management, real-time asset information, and offline capabilities for remote locations.

  • Tailored Workflows and Capabilities
    The multi-persona approach ensures each perspective user/technicians can have a tailored experience when navigating through the app. With the addition of the Field Service Technician persona, the app caters to off-site service orders, customer site visits, and remote troubleshooting—granting each persona the workflows and functionalities they need to perform efficiently.

  • Single Unified Mobile App
    Rather than deploying separate solutions for different roles, the SAP Maintenance Assistant mobile app offers a single, centralized platform capable of supporting multiple personas. This consolidation reduces complexity for IT teams, simplifies adoption and training, and provides a consistent user experience across different departments and job functions.

  • Licensing
    SAP Maintenance Assistant is licensed as a subscription-based software-as-a-service (SaaS) solution. Customers can purchase tiered user-based licenses through SAP Mobile Execution and Dynamic Forms material code, since the mobile application is included as part of this mobile bundle. 

Persona Introduction - Field Service Technician

In this blog, we provide some detailed information about the standard persona - Field Service Technician. 

                             

While field service technicians share many responsibilities with maintenance technicians—such as maintenance, repair, service, and asset installation—their focus is more customer-oriented. They typically travel to customer locations, manage van stock, perform their job at customer's location, log their time and various expenses, and prioritize customer feedback and satisfaction. This daily workflow contrasts with the more facility-based tasks of a maintenance technician.

In this blog, we take a closer look at the new Field Service Technician persona in the SAP Maintenance Assistant mobile app. We’ll explore how the app streamlines each stage of a field technician’s journey—from receiving the service orders and traveling to customer sites, to completing service tasks and obtaining on-site customer approval.

Process Flow

SAP Maintenance Assistant mobile app on Field Service Technician persona (on S4 Service Public Cloud) is designed to guide the technician seamlessly through the essential activities of the field service job. Key capabilities include:

  • Reviewing service orders and service items assigned for a day / week / month.
  • Reviewing pre-planned service expense and service materials on assigned jobs.
  • Accepting or rejecting tasks.
  • Indication of traveling an arriving at the customer's location.
  • Starting, holding, and completing service orders.
  • Recording expenses and used materials.
  • Capturing technician and customer's signatures if needed.
  • Generating service report for final confirmation.

Below is a flow diagram depicting how a user would execute an assigned task from start to complete. 

 

 

Application Features

Let's dive in deeper and see some key features supported as part of the new Field Service Technician Persona.

Home screen

The enhanced home screen offers technicians a consolidated view of all assigned tasks, with the flexibility to filter by day, week, or month—or to target specific status KPIs such as Not Yet Accepted, Not Yet Started, In Progress, and Completed. This allows for streamlined prioritization and efficient task management.

We’ve also introduced new UI object cards to increase visibility and enable more interactive workflows. Technicians can now directly accept or reject tasks, as well as create new service orders or confirmations—all without leaving the home screen. This holistic approach ensures a more intuitive and productive user experience for field service operations.

Service Order Details

The service order details screen provides technicians with a complete overview of all necessary information before starting a job. This includes quick access to the associated equipment or functional location, relevant notes, attached documents, and involved business parties. A consolidated view of pre-planned service items, expense items, and material items helps technicians efficiently plan and track required resources.

Key dates—such as requested start, end, and due dates—are readily visible for seamless scheduling. Technicians can also update their job status to reflect Accepted, Traveling, or Arrived at Customer Site. Additional capabilities include adding reference objects (equipment, functional locations, materials), creating or editing unplanned items, and generating service confirmations—all directly from the service order details screen. This integrated approach ensures technicians can manage every aspect of their tasks in one centralized location.

Service Order Items

Items form a critical component of any service order, which is why we’ve introduced a dedicated, table-based view for streamlined visibility and management. Technicians can review all relevant item details at a glance and directly edit fields—such as quantity, parent/high-level item, and accounting indicators—right in the table. This new interface significantly enhances preparation for upcoming tasks, enabling immediate adjustments and ensuring accurate updates.

Create Service Items

                                         Unexpected expenses or parts often arise during service visits. To accommodate these, the SAP Maintenance Assistant mobile app allows technicians to seamlessly add unplanned items—such as additional travel expenses or spare parts—to any service order. By specifying the item type, quantity, or amount, the new entry is instantly captured within the system and synced back to SAP S/4HANA Cloud (public edition) (when sync is initiated) to maintain accurate, up-to-date records.

Create Confirmation and Confirmation Items

Before finalizing a service order, technicians must log the actual time worked, any incurred expenses, and the parts used. To facilitate this, service confirmations can be created directly from the Service Order Details screen. When selecting the relevant items to confirm, technicians simply enter the actual job duration, expense amounts, or parts quantities. Multiple confirmations can be made for the same service order items, if needed, throughout the service process.

Note:

  • Items in completed status can no longer be confirmed.
  • The same service order items can be confirmed more than once if required.

Conclusion

The addition of the Field Service Technician persona to the SAP Maintenance Assistant mobile app marks an important milestone in delivering a truly holistic and flexible solution for Enterprise Asset Management. By tailoring workflows and interfaces specifically for off-site, customer-oriented tasks, this new persona helps technicians efficiently manage their daily assignments, capture real-time job information, and ensure a seamless user experience from start to finish. 

Looking ahead, our commitment remains steadfast in evolving the SAP Maintenance Assistant mobile app to address the diverse needs of various roles within maintenance and field service environments. We invite you to explore these new features, share your feedback, and stay tuned for further enhancements as we continue to refine and expand our multi-persona approach within SAPMaintenance Assistant.

The 2502 app is now available to download from the App Store or Google Play store with build in pre-laoded demo data, allowing you to check out the new Field ServiceTechnician person as well as new innovations for Maintenance Technician personas.

entry.title: Re: How to check if screen or popup exists in SAP Build P...
entry.content:
I use a TRY CATCH control, and set Timeout as the error to catch.

The automation waits for the pop up in order of closing it. If timeout error raises, the automation continues with the next step.

A disadvantage is that the code runs slower waiting for the Timeout error, if someone knows a better way, I'd be glad to know!

Click on no 

click on continue

click on continue

Give password of both system

click on continue

click on continue

now click on use

click on activate

Click on yes

6. Now we’ll check

entry.title: Re: ABAP ADT code completion gets crazy due to some code...
entry.content:
After some tests, I realize that the backend ABAP parser goes wrong if any line above contains one of any of these String Template wildcard characters:

|\n|

|\r|

|\{|

|\||

No issue with |\t| and |\\|.

If you get crazy with that bug, you may simply replace them respectively with:

|{ cl_abap_char_utilities=>newline }|

|{ cl_abap_char_utilities=>crlf(1) }|

|{ '{' }|

|{ '|' }|

entry.title: Writing mta.yaml for for python cloud foundry project
entry.content:
Prerequisites

Before you start, make sure you have the following: - A Cloud Foundry account - Cloud Foundry Command Line Interface (CLI) installed - Python installed on your local machine - Node.js and npm installed for building your MTA archive.

Step 1: Set Up Your Project Directory

Create a new directory for your project.

 

mkdir python-cf-mta-project cd python-cf-mta-project

 

Step 2: Initialize a new Python project

 

python -m venv venv

source venv/bin/activate # On Windows, use `venv\Scripts\activate`

pip install Flask

 

Step 3: Create a simple Python application

Create a file named `app.py` with the following content:

 

from flask import Flask

import os

from cfenv import AppEnv

app = Flask(__name__)

cf_port = os.getenv("PORT")

env = AppEnv()

@app.route('/')

def home():

return "Hello, Cloud Foundry!"

if cf_port is None:

    app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=8000, debug=False)

else:

    app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=int(cf_port), debug=False)This starts a web server at port 8080.

 

Step 4: Create the requirement.txt file

flask

cfenv==0.5.3

Step 5: Create the mta.yaml file as below 

 

ID: python-cf-mta 
_schema-version: 3.3.0 
version: 1.1.0 
description: "Python web service with MTA" 
modules: 
  - name: python-web-cf-mta 
    type: python 
    path: ./ 
    provides: 
      - name: srv-api 
        properties: srv-url: ${default-url} 
    parameters: 
        memory: 1024M 
        disk-quota: 1024M 
        buildpacks: - python_buildpack 
        commands: - python app.py

 

 

Step 6: Setup MTA Archive build tool

 

npm install -g mbt

 

Step 7: Build your MTA archive

 

mbt build

 

This command will generate a file named `python-cf-mta-project_0.0.1.mtar` in your project directory.

Step 8: Deploy to Cloud Foundry

Log in to your Cloud Foundry account.

cf login -a your_cf_api_url -u your_username -p your_password

Deploy your MTA archive.

cf deploy python-cf-mta-project_0.0.1.mtar

Step 9: Verify the deployment

Check the status of your application.cf appsVisit the URL provided by Cloud Foundry to ensure your application is running. 

Finish, you have created a python cloud foundry project with MTA.yaml instead of using manifest.yml. 

In this blog, we have walked through the process of creating a Python Cloud Foundry project with an `mta.yaml` file containing the build instructions. This process includes setting up your project directory, creating the necessary `mta.yaml` files, building the MTA archive, and deploying it to Cloud Foundry.

 

 

 

 

SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry runtime and environment

At the moment I am only able to read the jobs via the "Application Jobs" app, since I do not have S_APPL_LOG authorization for the object / subobject pair... which I created. This is weird. Seriously.

entry.title: Professional Services in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502
entry.content:
In this blog post I’d like to outline the innovations for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502 in the area of Professional Services. These are the topics that are covered:

  • Improving Project Steering with Project Financial Booklet – Professional Service app (J55)
  • Changing Document Currency in Preliminary Billing Documents for Professional Services (J14) 
  • Process Extensibility from Journal Entries to Billing Document (4E9, J14) 
  • Activation of New Resource-Related Intercompany Billing via SSCUI (16T, 4AN) 
  • Project Control – Professional Services Projects (J11) 
  • Integration of Information from SAP Success Factors (6JO)

Watch this video to get a glimpse of the new features:

Improving Project Steering with Project Financial Booklet – Professional Service app (J55)

The Project Financial Booklet – Professional Services app is a next generation multidimensional analytical tool tailored for professional service users. It offers an interactive and comprehensive view of the project financial KPIs by combining predefined business pages for different types of projects, namely customer projects, projects with revenue, and overhead projects, all within a one-stop shop. This facilitates fast decision-making for management. Users will be able to validate and monitor project-relevant financial data in one reporting app, gain an interactive and comprehensive view of project financial KPIs and support decision-making with real-time financial insights.

Concrete capabilities are:

  • Analyze financial key figures for different project types
  • View separate pages for customer projects, projects with revenue, and overhead projects
  • Get pre-defined views on financial data with pre-defined KPIs for each project type
  • Adapt rows and columns, and save user-specific views
  • Calculate new KPIs based on the pre-delivered KPIs
  • Export to PDF or Excel

For more information, see: Project Financial Booklet – Professional Service (Project Financial Booklet – Professional Services | SAP Help Portal)

Take a look at the demo to see how it looks in the system:

 

Changing Document Currency in Preliminary Billing Documents for Professional Services (J14) 

This feature has been available via feature toggle already since 2402 but is now generally available without having to activate it in the Activate Features app.

Changing the document currency provides the billing clerk more flexibility during the billing process and is particularly useful when customers request to be billed in a currency different from the one specified in the customer project, for example, because of high inflation.

Billing clerks can use the “Change Preliminary Billing Documents” app to change the document currency in the header details of a preliminary billing document for professional services. This significantly improves flexibility during the billing process and increases customer satisfaction as invoices can be billed in different currencies.

For this new functionality you can also have look on the details in this video:

For even more details, please have a look at the entry of this topic on SAP Help Portal.

Please remember that Andreas Hammerschmidt updates his detailed blog “Project Billing for Customer Projects in SAP S/4HANA Cloud” with every release, so you can find a comprehensive overview of the functionality there.

 

Process Extensibility from Journal Entries to Billing Document (4E9, J14)

Process extensibility is a very valuable tool to add more necessary information to to entire end to end process. Combined with additional options it can heavily influence the resource related billing to achieve the diverse process variations. As a result, resource-related billing processes can be tailored and optimized to meet the unique needs of each customer. You will be able to shape the aggregation criteria of the billing document and with additional enhancement options, the extensibility can be used to facilitate the split of invoice based on custom values and influence the sales rate determination (only in project billing) and surcharge/discount determination.

You want more? Take a look at this demo:

Activation of New Resource-Related Intercompany Billing via SSCUI (16T, 4AN)

Before the 2502 release the activation of new resource-related intercompany billing was possible via feature toggle. That feature toggle will now be replaced by an SSCUI. The SSCUI can be accessed via Manage Your Solution application. This will be helpful in running the separate solutions in different tenants, facilitating the customers to try the new solution

The default setting of SSCUI is to set to run old solution, like feature toggle. However, for the customers who have already activated the new solution, the SSCUI will be set to new solution for that tenant. Hence there would be no day – 1 Impact. Same as the feature toggle, once SSCUI is activated, cannot be reversed in that tenant.

Note: Feature toggle will be removed in future (planned release 2502.1)

Have a look at this demo to get a better understanding of the system:

 

Project Control – Professional Services Projects (J11)

You might be already familiar with this app, as it isn’t new itself. It provides one entry point for project managers that gives an overview of project-related information and access to related apps and actions.

With this 2502 release we bring project managers the additional possibility for direct insights into work packages of professional services projects through this app. This will give project managers information on planned and actual efforts, expenses, costs and revenue, blocked functions, related billing elements, planned and recognized revenue, purchase order related insights and much more.

 

For even more details, please have a look at the entry of this topic on SAP Help Portal.

Have a look at this demo to get a better understanding of the system:

 

Integration of Information from SAP Success Factors (6JO)

The scope item 6JO is part of the Cloud for Projects functionality on the BTP.

Resource Managers often miss actual information about the availabilities and absences from employees. To staff people to projects this information is crucial to ensure the project success. Changed availability of employees often require adjustments of staffing and planning. Resource Management now can interface daily availabilities from SAP Success Factors via iFlow, this allows accurate information about employee availabilities incl. vacation and sick leave, which facilitates more realistic planning and forecasting.

Let’s look closer at a concrete scenario: The resource Harrison Ford is present in a project in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition as well as the Resource Management functionality on BTP and SuccessFactors. The availability is maintained for Jan – Feb 2025 for Harrison Ford and is in sync for both systems. Now there is a new availability added for the month of March 2024 in SuccessFactors and an absence of 5 days for the month of February. When the iFLow is triggered to push the changes pushed and reflected in SAP Project and Resource Management and shown in the ‘My Resources’ app.

You can see how this scenario looks in the system right here:

 

For more information, see “SAP SuccessFactors Workforce Availability Integration with SAP Project and Resource Management”

 

Thanks for taking the time to read this blog.

If you are interested in the latest and greatest blogs on Professional Services, please have a look at my collection blog and also take a look at the Community page for Professional Services.

https://pages.community.sap.com/topics/s4hana-cloud-professional-services

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-sap/professional-services-in-the-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-the/ba-p/13620466

 

More Information

If you are interested in the latest and greatest blog posts on Enterprise Portfolio and Project Management, please have a look at my collection blog and also take a look at the Community page for Enterprise Portfolio and Project Management (Project Control) and also check out the blog post on Professional Services published by my colleague Katharina Stopf.

Don’t hesitate to leave a comment and check out our SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Community to ask your questions and engage with the experts. Follow the PSCC_Enablement tag to stay up to date with our latest blog posts.

 

Watch the Replays of Our SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2408 Early Release Series!

From July 8 to July 12, we hosted a series of 20 compelling live sessions to highlight the exciting innovations shipped with the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2408 release. Missed the live sessions? We've got you covered! Take advantage of the entire series or pick and choose the sessions most relevant to you. You will find a 30-minute-long recording and a presentation for Enterprise Portfolio and Project Management as well as 19 other topics of interest. Don't miss this chance and watch them on demand – anywhere, anytime.

SAP S/4HANA Cloud is the foundation of the intelligent, sustainable enterprise and is an innovative, robust, and scalable ERP. We at Cloud ERP Product Success offer a service as versatile as our product itself. Check out the numerous offerings our team has created for you below:

entry.title: SAPUI5 & OData: Multiple service calls vs single service call with multiple entities
entry.content:
Hi,

We have a requirement to create a SAPUI5 application that will display data from multiple mainly non-related tables using OData V2. We'll be using VSCode to create the UI5 application.

Is it better to:

a) create a separate OData service for each table and have multiple datasources in the manifest.json

or

b) one OData service containing multiple entities (each entity would relate to an individual DB table) ?

If the answer is b) then how do we structure the OData service and related manifest.json to retrieve all the required data from the one OData service?

All help greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Currency Conversion issue in SAC
entry.content:
Dear All, 

Can anyone assist me with currency conversion? I’ve tried every possible solution, but nothing seems to work.

I've created a model with the following measures and dimensions:

  • Measures: Amount
  • Dimensions: Version, Account, Product, Organization, Date

Additionally, I have a currency table labeled SAC_CURRENCY. The model

currently has a single version (Actual), and the Account dimension in focus is Cost of Goods Sold (COGS).

After summing up all child GL accounts and performing the currency conversion to USD, the expected value for COGS should be 3,104,631,397. However, the model is showing an incorrect value of 4,096.74 million, which is significantly off.

Can someone help identify what might be causing this issue? why is this happening please refer screenshots which will be helful in identifying what's causing this why it's appearing incorrect even when taking default value as fixed that is usd or taken from dimension 

 

the import file data 

 

Version 

Account 

date is 2018 till 2019

Product 

Organization 

Model preferences 

currency table 

after doing all settings nor cross caluculation tab is appearing

entry.title: How to add days to an existing date via advanced formulae
entry.content:
Hi - I need to add 84 days to an existing date in a model.

I am using 

MEMBERSET [d/Measures] = "Quantity"
MEMBERSET [d/Last_Modified_Date] = "2025-02-11"

DATA()=RESULTLOOKUP([d/GestationDate]=NEXT(84,"DAY",[d/Close_Date]))

However running this has no effect.

I only want to update any GestationDate where the Last_Modified_Date = '2025-02-11' (or preferably use the TODAY() function somehow ) with whatever is in the Close_Date + 84 days.

I have seen a couple of threads on this and from those I tried this but it has no effect when I run the data action.

Am I trying to perform the impossible?

The whole reason I am doing this is because I am using an API to push data into this model and there is no wrangling facility available or it would be a simple case of creating a calculated column of [GestationDate]=DATEADD([d/Close_Date],84,"day") and loading it in.

Also - I cannot add this calculated field to the source.

I was thinking if I can get this script to run on the hour a couple of minutes after the api runs it would do the trick.

Any help much appreciated.

2️⃣ Managing multiple capacities in a work center – Since CM25 allowed distributing operations across multiple capacities while adjusting the planned finish date, does Public Cloud provide an equivalent approach to ensure that ATP reflects the correct availability date?

3️⃣ Integration with HR work schedules – If Public Cloud does not support direct integration with HR work schedules for capacity availability, is there an alternative way to ensure that employee absences (e.g., sick leave, vacation) are considered in capacity planning?

I would appreciate any further insights or recommendations on these topics. Thanks again for your support!

Best regards,

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

entry.title: Decentralized EWM on an SAP S/4HANA stack
entry.content:
Dears,

I would like to understand, what would be the possible impact of changing below configuration on already running S/4HANA on-premise 2021?

SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Enable Decentralized EWM

It is important to clarify, that we ARE NOT using EWM (neither Emended nor Decentralized), but for some reason, during postings in MIGO transaction, system is "looking for" EWM tables to update (checked in de-bug mode). During this time of approximate 30 mins, documents posted are locked in SM12. We found out, IF Decentralized EWM is activated in SPRO, the system skip searching and directly post without DB block.

Help information says:

Note:

You must not change the configuration of whether EWM is embedded or decentralized once transaction data exists, because this would disrupt existing processes and documents.

For more information about EWM deployment options, see SAP Note 1606493.

So, we have transactional data, BUT it is not in EWM tables (as we didn't activate EWM), hence question is: In case, we change set up to "Decentralized EWM", when we actually don't have such, whether to expect any negative impact on system?

Thank you!

I want to overwrite my result table as the union of itself with the new record, but the syntax check fails as a "from" clause is expected implying that I have to use a table. I know that what I'm trying to achieve is possible on other DBs and SQL flavors but cannot seem to find the equivalent on HANA. Any ideas? I have attached a screenshot of the code snippet.

Thanks!

entry.title: The Broad Spectrum of Supply Chain Security
entry.content:
By Jay Thoden van Velzen, Technical Advisor, Office of the CSO

This blog discusses topics also covered in The Future of Supply Chain podcast, Episode 83: Supply Chain Security, Scope and Definitions. If you prefer podcasts over blogs, click the link!

 

Supply Chain Security From Different Viewpoints

There is increasing understanding that supply chain security is important to the resilience of business operations. However, since it covers many different aspects, we often misunderstand each other. The problem is that supply chain security means very different things depending on your functional area and from which viewpoint you look at it. Supply chain security covers a broad spectrum, and the term is used for widely different challenges. This leads to confusion, and people using metrics from one area to make an argument in another. It is not that simple.

To an operational business executive keeping a product line going, supply chain security refers to the physical movement of goods and components. To enterprise IT leaders, it is who and how contractors access company systems and facilities, and what technology providers they bring in. To software developers, supply chain security refers to their software dependency chain. When they discuss their supply chain challenges, they will have trouble understanding each other.

In all cases, we’re dealing with suppliers. But the reduction of risk in each area requires a very different approach. SAP, as a provider of business cloud solutions, touches on the entire spectrum of supply chain security. Hopefully, our broad experience can help get our three leaders on the same page.

 

Surveying the Spectrum

In broad outlines, we can distinguish between three main categories:

  • Physical Supply Chain Security
  • Digital Supply Chain Security
  • Software Supply Chain Security

The cases mentioned above fit rather neatly into those categories. However, there is also significant overlap between these categories, and it is therefore better to refer to supply chain security as a continuing spectrum. Just how in a color spectrum we can still distinguish between red, yellow or blue even though the scale is continuous, so are these three markers still useful to help us navigate the field.

Physical Supply Chain Security

At the outer edge of the spectrum, we see the risks to the supply chain with direct physical, material impact. Resilience must be built into the supply chain to withstand the impact of conflict, natural disasters, or sanctions. In recent years, we have seen pandemic restrictions on different regions at different times have significant cascading impact on supply chains. Conflicts have made major transport routes less safe or cut them off. Sanctions have forced re-alignment of energy supply lines and geopolitical tensions have forced relocation of semiconductor manufacturing and restricted technology transfer- and export regimes.

Another example in the physical realm is product tampering. How are products – whether supplies or finished products –protected from being tampered with and protected from theft in storage depots and during transit? Even if you have taken appropriate measures in your own facilities, inevitably there are spots in your supply chain you don’t have direct control over.

Aside from tampering, a similar problem comes up with component integrity. Not all suppliers operate with the same integrity, and may offer common electronic components, for instance, that are not up to standard or code. Using such components in consumer electronics can have health and safety consequences through malfunction.

Digital Supply Chain Security

Component integrity is also a digital problem. Such components are listed on a digital marketplace, with seemingly correct standard dimensions and specification, offered by a company that changes its name every 6 months. Verifying that the marketplace performs due diligence on the listed suppliers and their product offerings falls under digital supply chain security.

A key digital supply chain security challenge is the provisioning and monitoring of access to your organization’s systems, networks and facilities by third party contractors and service providers. These can be employees of a system integrator that help manage IT systems but also includes third party facility maintenance or cleaning staff. Such contractors are often issued access badges and credentials to internal systems to conduct their work. Others are treated as visitors and may need to be always accompanied by an employee host.

For managed service providers (MSP) and Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) scenarios, the dynamic reverses. Instead of provisioning access to third-party staff to systems you control, now your organization uses systems operated by a third-party supplier. This could be a transition from self-hosted e-mail servers operated by contractors to a cloud-based e-mail service, or from on-premise SAP ERP systems to SAP cloud ERP services.

Software Supply Chain Security

The final category covers the software that your organization chooses to use or include into your products and services. This runs from SaaS through Platform- and Infrastructure-as-a-Service (PaaS, IaaS), to commercial software solutions deployed in your IT landscape. This requires verification that such suppliers meet your security and compliance requirements, have effective security programs in place, and fix vulnerabilities and misconfigurations within an acceptable time frame.

It also includes any open source software (OSS) solutions that you use in your organization, or libraries you include in your own development projects. OSS components often help reduce cost and accelerate development, but they come with their own risks. Only the largest OSS projects can match the level of support commercial providers offer for their solutions. Strictly speaking, there is no supplier in OSS, unless you select an enterprise license or service contract. Many OSS components themselves have dependencies on other OSS projects. Managing OSS dependency chains and keeping components up to date yourself require significant effort that is often underestimated.

Software supply chain concerns have raised to the level of governmental policy and regulation.

 

Mitigating Supply Chain Security Risks

Leaving aside any physical security measures, we can reduce supply chain security risks with these mitigation approaches:

  • Business Planning and Supply Chain Resilience
  • Third Party Risk Management (TPRM) and Compliance
  • IAM and Zero Trust
  • Vulnerability and Patch Management

As shown in the diagram below, they apply differently across the risk spectrum.

Business Planning and Supply Chain Resilience

Business Planning and Supply Chain Resilience – and planning for supply chain resilience – is critical to manage an ever-shifting portfolio of suppliers of goods and services across different jurisdictions, customs controls, and tax regimes. Security risks to the supply chain must be included alongside other business risks in business planning, while resilience must be baked into the supply chain to have the agility to adjust when circumstances beyond your control require you to.

SAP has solutions in its portfolio, such as SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain and other SAP Supply Chain Management Software, SAP Business Network, or SAP International Trade Management, that can help with this.

The bar for this in the diagram extends to everywhere in the supply chain where we have suppliers, as well as the impact of geopolitics.

Third Party Risk Management and Compliance

TPRM and compliance verification are necessary to ensure that your goods and service providers conduct business in a way that meets your security and compliance requirements, as well as risk appetite. This includes the verification of audit reports from suppliers, but also the maturity of their security programs. It covers the integrity and reliability of the supplier and its leaders, adherence to ethical business practices, and the supplier’s likelihood to deliver on its commitments. Such assessments extend to the supplier’s suppliers, as well. Ultimately, we must decide whether any supplier is one you’re willing to do business with.

The bar for this in the diagram extends to everywhere in the supply chain where we have suppliers.

IAM and Zero Trust

IAM and Zero Trust governs identity and access to internal systems as well as managed services and cloud applications, both for employees and contractors. Zero Trust involves features such as Multi Factor Authentication (MFA), device identity, and network-, attribute- and policy-based access controls. The topic goes far beyond the scope of this blog, but the NIST Special Publication 800-207 on Zero Trust Architecture is a readable introduction and guide.

The bar in the diagram for this extends further to the right than in the previous two, despite the lack of suppliers in open source. This is to cover OSS applications or solutions you have built yourself from OSS but still need included in your authentication and authorization scope. It starts where you provide access to third parties to your systems and facilities.

Vulnerability and Patch Management

For whatever software you use, whether managed by a service provider or operated yourself, you have a responsibility for vulnerability and patch management – be it with increasing responsibilities the further right in the spectrum we go. For MSPs and SaaS solutions, you have an obligation to verify that your suppliers are meeting their commitments. For commercial and open source software you operate yourself, you are fully responsible for keeping the software up to date when patches become available, and that the software is configured securely.

Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) is often associated with software supply chain management, but I think it is better associated with Third Party Risk Management and Compliance. After all, it is intended to give customers a means to conduct their risk assessment in using the software or service of that supplier.  

For the provider of the software or service, the key strategy is Vulnerability and Patch Management. If those processes work well, the SBOM – including its software dependencies – will be of better quality and therefore make a stronger impression on your customers in their TPRM evaluation.

 

Cross-Functional Effort

From these approaches, it is clear that these will be covered by different functional areas in the organization. Business planning falls under business operations. TPRM and compliance under Governance, Risk and Compliance (GRC), while IAM and Zero Trust and vulnerability management cover IT, cybersecurity and development teams. Covering supply chain security risk is therefore a cross-functional effort.

None of these approaches are easy. But there are suppliers, including SAP, that can support you in that journey.

 

More Information

More information can be found through the following links:

  • The Future of Supply Chain podcast, Episode 83: Supply Chain Security, Scope and Definitions
  • Government’s Role in Increasing Software Supply Chain Security: A Toolbox for Policy Makers (interface, March 2023)
  • SAP Supply Chain Management Software
  • SAP Business Network
  • SAP International Trade Management
  • NIST Special Publication 800-207 on Zero Trust Architecture

entry.title: 2H 2024 SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding enhancement to the Internal Hire process.
entry.content:
Overview:

With 2H 2024, SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding has introduced additional enhancements to the Internal Hire process. These updates bring workflow support, cross-entity rule support, and position updates for internal hires initiated from SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting.

This blog explains the configuration options, validation mechanisms, and best practices for maximizing the benefits of these enhancements, ensuring a smoother and more efficient internal hiring experience.

Look back at the 2H 2023 release when we made the feature “Enable Enhanced Internal Hire Process” Automatically on. This feature leverages capabilities of Centralized Services in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central, offering data consistency between Recruiting and Employee Central.

Let’s roll back to the 2H 2024 release when we released the additional feature “Enable Additional Internal Hire Activities” to provide such as workflow support, cross-entity rule support, and position updates for internal hires initiated from SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting.

These switches are available in the Admin Center -> Onboarding General Settings page.

These switches are applicable only for internal hires initiated from the SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting flow.

Let us deep dive into these configuration options, validation mechanisms, and best practices for maximizing the benefits.

Let me refer to Switch 1 and Switch 2 as below.

Switch 1: Enable Enhanced Internal Hire Process

Switch 2: Enable Additional Internal Hire Activities

 

2H 2023

2H 2024

Configuration Switches

Switch 1 is made Automatically ON and you must keep this switch ON.

Note: This switch will be universal in 1H 2025.

You can refer blog for details https://community.sap.com/t5/human-capital-management-blogs-by-sap/elevating-internal-transfers-transitions-through-sap-successfactors/ba-p/13579221

 

Switch 2 is added to support functionalities such as workflow support, cross-entity rule support, and position updates which were not available with Switch 1.

If you wish to use workflow support, cross-entity rule support, and position updates then you must switch on both (Switch 1 +  Switch 2)

Recruit to hire data mapping

RCM-mapped data is saved in EC  on successful validation. Existing EC data will be retained if Data is not mapped from RCM.

RCM-mapped data is saved in EC  on successful validation. Existing EC data will be retained if Data is not mapped from RCM.

Data Validations

Data validation and Data saving for HRIS entities are supported by Centralized Services in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central.

The process aligns with MSS UI save and validation ensuring all necessary validations are triggered before initiating cross-boarding.

 

Data validation and Data saving for HRIS entities are supported by Centralized Services in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central

The process aligns with the Recruiting - Employee Central (EC) internal hire flow, ensuring all necessary validations are triggered before initiating cross-boarding.

Functionalities

Workflow support, cross-entity rule support, and position updates for internal hires are not supported with Switch 1 “ ON”.

Workflow support, cross-entity rule support, and position updates for internal hires are supported with (Switch 1 + Switch 2) “ ON”.

Rule Context

Rule Context should be set up as “Onboarding” = Yes and the rest other as “No”.

 

Rule Context should be set up as “ESS/MSS = Yes” regardless of the other contexts

Note: The existing rule with “ESS/MSS = Yes” will get executed for Initiate Cross boarding.

If you want to execute rules for ESS/MSS UI’s but not for crossboarding then please review and change the rule conditions to avoid validation errors.

You can set rule context “ ESS/MSS = No” if rules are not expected to be executed for Crossboarding or ESS/MSS UI’s.

Frequently asked questions:

1. Why are validation errors shown on the initiate onboarding for internal hire after enabling switch 1 or switch 2?

Ans: With this enhancement, EC HRIS entity data save is done using Centralized services in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central which validates data consistency between Recruiting and Employee Central. To start the Internal Hire Process successfully, with the workflow and position follow-up updates, you need to ensure all required validations are met. This includes position-related checks and other EC Entities Onsave or Onchange rules execution.

2. What are possible ways to check and resolve position validation errors?

Ans: Please refer to the below example for error and possible resolution

  • Error: "[jobInfo]: You have selected a position that can only be assigned to one employee at a time."
    Solution: Verify the target position and adjust the start date or FTE (Full-Time Equivalent) accordingly.
  • Error: "Assigning this employee to the selected position would result in multiple incumbents on two occasions during the period from … until … Please choose another position."
    Solution: Check the target position and ensure it is available for the intended start date.

3. What are possible ways to check and resolve Pay Component Recurring Errors?

  • Error: Crossboarding failures due to incorrect pay component mappings.
  • Solution: Refer to the following Help guide and KBA articles to ensure correct pay component mapping.
    • Mapping Data from Recruiting Management with Employee Central Entities | SAP Help Portal
    • https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3204900

Conclusion:

The "Enable  Additional Internal Hire Activities" switch ON ensures alignment with the Recruiting-Employee Central (EC) internal hire flow, triggering all necessary validations before initiating the process.

Manages data persistence and data validation between Recruiting and Employee Central using  Centralized services in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central enhances validation accuracy.

Backend data validation in Onboarding is improved using Centralized Services functionalities in Employee Central.

entry.title: Maximum double value in report appears to be 1.7E306 instead of the expected 1.7E308. Why is that?
entry.content:
Crystal reports for visual studio, service pack 37

My report accesses a database with a column whose type is double.

The maximum double value according to IEEE format is 1.79769313486231E+308

However, the maximum value is can display in my report is 1.79769313486231E+306

Note I'm displaying it in default format: 1.80e+306

If the value is any larger, my display is 1.#Je+000

If I attempt to pass the larger value to UFL function whose input param is double, the value passed is infinity.

As a test and to eliminate my database and UFL from the suspect list, I created a formula that simply returns a number.  Since I could not figure out how to specify a value using exponential notation in the formula I used the exp function which looks like:

numbervar num;

num := Exp(705.1775427);

That value is approximately 1.79769313486231E+306.  If I try to increase the 705.1775427 param any higher, crystal gives me a 'numeric overflow' when saving the formula.

It appears that Crystal is artificially limiting my maximum value.  Is that true and why?

entry.title: Re: API Testing with Tricentis Test Automation integrated with SAP Cloud ALM & SAP Test Automati
entry.content:
Dear Andy, yes it is possible. 

You should decouple this call into 2 API calls:

  1. Capture the access_token from the authentication response and put it on a Buffer.
  2. Use {access_token} or {B[access_token]} in subsequent API calls (e.g., Authorization header).
